name: San Francisco Mountains
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding in the high desert of southwestern Utah. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony nodules in volcanic terrain with expansive mountain views.
description:
The San Francisco Mountains in Beaver County offer excellent rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land without requiring permits or fees. This remote location sits within Utah's Basin and Range province at elevation, providing a scenic backdrop for amateur and experienced collectors alike. The volcanic terrain is rich with nodules and specimens of agate, jasper, and chalcedony scattered across washes and hillsides. Access is straightforward on public lands, making this an ideal destination for those seeking free, unrestricted rockhounding in a relatively untouched area.

The geology here reflects Miocene-era volcanic activity that deposited silica-rich materials throughout the region. As volcanic rocks weathered and fractured over millions of years, silica-bearing fluids infiltrated cavities and created the nodules you'll find today. The San Francisco Mountains themselves are a prominent volcanic complex, and the surrounding bajadas and washes expose material from this ancient volcanism. Collectors often find nodules in stream drainages where erosion naturally concentrates specimens.

Expect to explore open desert with scattered vegetation and exposed rock outcrops. The terrain is gently rolling to moderately steep in places, with good visibility for spotting material. Most productive areas are within reasonable walking distance of parking spots along roads accessing the area. Early morning or late afternoon visits let you maximize daylight and avoid midday heat during warmer months.
history: The San Francisco Mountains are part of a major Miocene volcanic field in southwestern Utah that formed roughly 20-30 million years ago. Extensive felsic volcanism and silica-rich hydrothermal alteration created the conditions for widespread agate and jasper nodule formation throughout this region, making it a natural repository of collectible specimens.
tips: Search washes and arroyos where water erosion has exposed and concentrated nodules - these natural collection points often yield the best specimens. Look for the characteristic banding and color patterns of agate nodules, which typically range from 1 to 4 inches across; darker bands of brown, red, and gray are common here. Bring a small magnifying glass to examine specimens for quality before collecting, since many nodules have worthless outer rinds but beautiful interiors. Early season spring rains can expose fresh material, so visit after seasonal wet weather for the best finds.
terrain_notes: Elevation ranges from 5,500 to 6,500 feet in the collecting areas. Terrain consists of open desert scrubland with scattered pinyon-juniper vegetation at higher elevations. Most rockhounding is done on gentle to moderate slopes with exposed washes and bajadas. Walking distances from parking areas typically range from 0.5 to 2 miles. Footing is uneven with scattered rocks and loose material underfoot; sturdy hiking boots are recommended.
best_season: April - June and September - November. Avoid December through February when snow can block access and make collecting uncomfortable. July and August bring intense heat exceeding 90 degrees Fahrenheit, making midday collecting difficult. Spring and fall offer ideal temperatures, good visibility, and reasonable chance of finding recently exposed material.
written_directions: From Beaver, Utah, take Utah State Route 153 south toward Beaver Canyon approximately 12 miles. Continue on Forest Service roads in the area south of Beaver toward the San Francisco Mountains volcanic field. GPS coordinates 38.459145, -113.306706 mark the primary rockhounding zone. Park at pullouts along accessed roads and walk into designated collecting areas. Four-wheel-drive may help access more remote sections during wet weather, but standard passenger vehicles can reach main collecting areas in dry conditions.
road_conditions: Graded dirt and gravel roads with possible washboard texture. Adequate clearance for passenger vehicles in normal conditions. Some spur roads may require high-clearance or four-wheel-drive after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along access roads. No developed parking lot. Park in wide areas along road edges away from active traffic. Capacity varies but generally accommodates 2-5 vehicles per pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Collect only loose material and weathered nodules. Do not dig into solid rock or disturb archaeological or historical sites. Leave habitat undisturbed.
rules:

nearest_services: Beaver, Utah has gas stations, restaurants, and convenience stores approximately 15-18 miles north. Water and emergency services available in Beaver.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain for most hikers. Uneven rocky ground and loose material underfoot require sturdy boots but not technical hiking skills. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Parking pullouts are accessible via standard roads. Distances from parking to productive areas are short, typically under 2 miles. Moderate elevation (5,500-6,500 feet) may affect those with altitude sensitivity.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find at San Francisco Mountains in Utah?
faq.answer: The San Francisco Mountains yield primarily chalcedonic nodules and banded agates with colors ranging from brown, red, and gray to occasional cream and white bands. Nodules typically range from 1 to 4 inches in diameter. You'll also find jasper material and other quartz varieties. Quality varies, so examine specimens closely before collecting, as many nodules have plain outer rinds but attractive banded or colored interiors.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at San Francisco Mountains near Beaver, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required. San Francisco Mountains sit on public BLM land where recreational rockhounding is allowed under FLPMA guidelines. Simply follow the personal use limit of 25 lbs per day and collect only loose nodules - no digging or power tools. Commercial collection is prohibited.

faq.question: Is San Francisco Mountains rockhounding site beginner-friendly and good for kids?
faq.answer: Yes, this site is excellent for beginners and families with children ages 6 and up. The terrain is relatively gentle with short walking distances from parking areas, and the nodules are easy to spot in open desert. It requires no special skills or equipment beyond a good hiking boot and basic rockhounding tools. The remote high-desert setting with mountain views also makes it an enjoyable outdoor experience for families.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for agate at San Francisco Mountains?
faq.answer: The best seasons are April through June and September through November. Spring and fall offer comfortable temperatures and good visibility. Avoid July and August when heat exceeds 90 degrees Fahrenheit, and December through February when snow may block access. Spring rains can expose fresh material, so visiting shortly after wet weather increases your chances of finding quality specimens.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at this Utah location?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for uneven terrain, a small hand pick or flathead screwdriver for extracting nodules, a collection bag or backpack, and a magnifying glass to examine specimens before collecting. A small hammer can help, but heavy excavation tools are not needed since most productive material comes from washes and loose areas. Sun protection, plenty of water, and a map or GPS device are essential for this remote area.

faq.question: How far is San Francisco Mountains rockhounding site from Beaver, Utah, and what are road conditions?
faq.answer: The site is approximately 15-18 miles south of Beaver via Utah Route 153 and Forest Service roads leading into the San Francisco Mountains volcanic field. Roads are graded dirt and gravel with possible washboard surfaces. Passenger vehicles can access main collecting areas in dry conditions, though four-wheel-drive may be helpful after rain. Check conditions before visiting and allow 30-45 minutes driving time from town.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at San Francisco Mountains in Utah. Free BLM rockhounding near Beaver. No permit needed. 25 lbs per day limit.
